script:
Leo: Last time we traced how a Gemma change travels from an idea to a public release, and we ended on a line Sara kept repeating, that a lot of model building is really evals. Today I want to go one level deeper into the exact part an infrastructure engineer like Frank will own, which is the eval and serving plumbing. Here is the thing that genuinely surprised me. The team has a compliant model coming together, but nothing ships until they can evaluate it, and right now the way they run those evals is held together by tribal knowledge and one very long document.

Maya: Hold on. If the hard part is building the model, why would evaluating it be the bottleneck? Evaluation sounds like the easy half. You have a model, you run some tests, you read the score. Where is the difficulty in that?

Leo: That intuition is exactly what I want to break, because it is wrong in a way that costs the team weeks. Building the model and running the model are two different problems. The model is a set of weights sitting in a checkpoint. To get a score out of it, you have to stand the model up as a running service, point an evaluation harness at it, feed it the right tasks in the right format, collect what it did, and grade it. Every one of those steps is a piece of infrastructure, and every one of them can break independently of the model being good or bad.

Maya: Okay, but people evaluate models all the time. Why is it brittle here specifically?

Leo: Because the current setup is a fifteen page document of manual steps. Somebody wrote down, in prose, how to spin up the machine, how to install the code, which flags to pass, which endpoint to hit, and where to copy the results. When a document is the source of truth, three bad things happen. People do the steps slightly differently, so two engineers get two different scores from the same model. Steps rot, because a version changes and nobody updates page nine. And onboarding a new person takes two weeks of somebody senior sitting next to them. So the evals are the bottleneck not because scoring is hard math, but because the act of running them reliably is unowned plumbing.

Maya: Fine, I will grant that the plumbing is messy. But how does messy plumbing actually change the eval outcome? A messy process that still produces the right number is annoying, not dangerous.

Leo: That is the key question, and the answer is that it does not always produce the right number. If one engineer runs the model through one serving path and another runs it through a different path, or on a slightly different code version, the scores disagree, and now you cannot tell whether a model change helped or whether the harness moved under you. When you cannot trust the number, you cannot make the go or no go decision on a release. So the messiness leaks directly into the decision. That is why fixing it is not cleanup, it is unblocking the whole team.

Maya: Alright. You said there are harnesses. What actually runs the evals?

Leo: There are two eval worlds, and they are worth separating carefully. The first is called Gbench. Think of it as a benchmark harness that lives in a code repository. It runs two kinds of things. The first kind is the classic academic evals, the single question in, single answer out benchmarks. The second kind is the interesting one, agentic tasks.

Maya: Agentic tasks. What does Gbench do differently for those?

Leo: For an agentic task, Gbench does not just ask the model a question. It stands up a small fake world for the model to act in. It creates fake files, fake tools, and seeds them with known data. Then it triggers the model as an agent and lets it take actions inside that sandbox. When the agent is done, Gbench inspects the resulting state of that fake world and grades based on what changed.

Maya: Wait, why grade the world instead of grading what the model said? That sounds like more work for the same answer.

Leo: It is more work, and it is a completely different answer. Here is a concrete example. Suppose the task is, cancel the customer's second order and refund it. A text grader would read the model's reply and check whether it said something that sounds like a cancellation. But the model can say all the right words and never actually call the cancel tool. If you instead seed a fake orders database, let the agent run, and then look at the database afterward, you can check the truth. Is the second order actually marked cancelled. Was a refund actually recorded. You are grading what the model did to a backend, not the sentence it emitted.

Maya: So the text can lie and the state cannot.

Leo: Exactly. The state is the ground truth. This is not a random idea, it comes straight out of the research the team leans on. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard version three and the tau-bench work both pushed this shift, from grading the words to grading the effect on an environment. For tools and agents, the sentence is theater. The action is what matters. And that is why these agentic evals catch failures the academic single answer benchmarks are blind to.

Maya: Before we leave Gbench, how does it seed that fake world so the grading is fair? If the sandbox is different every time, the score is noise.

Leo: Right, and that is why seeding is deliberate. Gbench starts the fake world from a fixed, known state. The same fake files, the same fake tools, the same starting rows in that fake orders database, every run. Because the start is fixed and the correct end state is defined up front, the grader can compare the world the agent produced against the world it should have produced. If the seed drifted run to run, you could not tell a model failure from a setup difference, so pinning the starting state is part of what makes the eval trustworthy in the first place.

Maya: So the fixed seed is doing the same job for Gbench that reproducible infrastructure does for the whole pipeline.

Leo: Exactly the same job, one level down. Fix the inputs, define the correct outputs, and any change in the score has to come from the model, not from the environment shifting underfoot. That principle, hold the setup constant so the signal is real, is the thread running through this entire lesson.

Maya: Got it. That is Gbench. What is the second world?

Leo: The second world is called Ditto. Ditto is an internal eval runner. It evaluates a model in the team's own internal format, and instead of loading the weights itself, it evaluates by pinging a model API. So Ditto sends requests to an endpoint, gets responses back, and scores them. The catch is where Ditto runs and where the model lives.

Maya: Why would that matter? A request is a request.

Leo: It matters because of two different homes. Ditto runs on Borg. Borg is Google's internal cluster scheduler, the thing that decides where jobs run inside Google's own data centers. But the model under test is hosted on GCP, which is Google Cloud, the external cloud product. So you have a Borg job that has to reach out and call an endpoint living on GCP. That Borg to GCP crossover is a real boundary, with its own networking, its own identity and permissions, and its own failure modes.

Maya: And let me guess, that boundary is where something is stuck.

Leo: That is exactly where a teammate named Sindhu is stuck right now. The task is to get the Hugging Face version of the model deployed on GCP with an API, so that Ditto, sitting on Borg, can call it. The model math is not the problem. The problem is the crossover, standing up the hosted endpoint and wiring the permissions so a Borg job is allowed to talk to it. If that link does not work, Ditto cannot reach the model, and the entire internal eval suite cannot run.

Maya: So a networking problem is holding up the evaluation of a model. That still feels backwards to me. Can you connect it? Why does a hosting issue gate the model work?

Leo: Follow the chain. The team is building a compliant model. To know if the compliant model is any good, they need the internal evals. The internal evals run through Ditto. Ditto only produces a score if it can call the model endpoint. The endpoint only exists if the model is correctly hosted on GCP and reachable from Borg. So the hosting link is the first domino. If it is down, every domino behind it, the score, the go or no go, the release, is frozen. That is how an unglamorous networking task ends up on the critical path for a frontier model.

Maya: Okay, that finally lands. Now you mentioned the model is served in different ways. Say more, because you keep saying serving path and I want to know why it is not just one thing.

Leo: Good, because this is the part people skip. The same set of weights can be run by different serving software, and the two do not automatically behave identically. The development model is exercised through two surfaces. One is vLLM, which is a high throughput serving engine built for speed. The other is the Hugging Face implementation, the more reference style PyTorch version. Same weights, two runtimes.

Maya: If it is the same weights, why would it matter which runtime you use? Weights are weights.

Leo: Because a runtime is more than the weights. It includes how the prompt gets formatted, how tokens get sampled, and how batching and numerical details are handled. Small differences there can move outputs, especially on tool calling and multi turn behavior. So if your eval hits vLLM but your users run the Hugging Face path, or the reverse, your score describes a model your users never touch. The eval has to hit the runtime you actually care about certifying. That is why the serving surface is not a detail. It decides which model you are really measuring.

Maya: So there are two harnesses, two homes, and two runtimes, and they all have to line up.

Leo: They all have to line up, every single run, or the number is not trustworthy. Which brings us to the punchline, and to where Frank actually plugs in. Today all of that lining up is done by hand, from that fifteen page document. Frank's contribution is to turn the whole thing into reproducible one-shot runs.

Maya: One-shot runs. Define that for me, concretely. What actually happens when someone kicks one off?

Leo: Picture a single command that does the entire dance and then cleans up after itself. First, Terraform provisions the resources. Terraform is infrastructure as code, meaning you describe the machines and services you want in a file, and the tool makes reality match the file. Second, a Docker image pins the code. Docker packages the exact software and dependencies into an image, and critically it pins a specific Gemmaclaw benchmark commit, so you know precisely which version of the eval you ran. Third, Cloud Run executes one job against one model build. Cloud Run is a service that runs a container to completion and then stops. Fourth, the results are written out to durable storage, somewhere that outlives the job. And fifth, everything tears down, so you are not paying for idle machines or leaving state around to rot.

Maya: That is a lot of moving parts to replace a document. Why is that actually better? Convince me it is not just fashion.

Leo: Three concrete reasons, and each one maps to a failure we already talked about. First, identical reproducible environments every time. Because the environment is built from code, not assembled by hand, two people running the same command get the same environment, so their scores are comparable. That kills the problem of two engineers getting two different numbers. Second, a diff you can read before you apply it. Terraform will show you exactly what it is about to change before it changes anything, so you review the change the way you review code, instead of discovering it in production. Third, automatic rollback when something breaks. If a run fails partway, the system can return to the known good state instead of leaving a half built mess for a human to untangle.

Maya: Wait, walk me through the rollback part, because that is the one that sounds too good. What does automatic rollback actually protect me from?

Leo: Picture the old way. You are halfway through the manual steps, the machine is provisioned, the code is half installed, and then a step fails. Now you have a broken, half built environment, and a human has to log in and figure out what got created and tear it down by hand. With infrastructure as code, the system already knows the exact set of resources it created, so on failure it can reverse them and return you to the last known good state on its own. You never end up staring at a mystery pile of half provisioned machines wondering which ones are safe to delete.

Maya: So rollback is really about never being left in an in between state you have to untangle.

Leo: That is precisely it. The environment is either fully built or fully gone, never stuck in the middle. And that same property is why the diff before you apply matters so much. You see the full set of changes as one reviewable list, you approve it, it applies as a unit, and if anything goes wrong it unwinds as a unit. The run stops being a fragile sequence of hopeful manual steps and becomes one atomic, reviewable operation.

Maya: And the fifteen page document?

Leo: The document dies, and that is the whole point. Every manual step in it becomes a line of code that anyone can run. The tribal knowledge that used to live in one senior engineer's head becomes something a new person can execute on day one. That is what infrastructure as code really buys you. It is not that the machines are fancier. It is that the setup is now readable, reviewable, repeatable, and owned, instead of being an oral tradition.

Maya: Let me push on one more thing before I buy the whole story. Why is this the highest leverage place for an infrastructure engineer? Frank could go learn the training math instead. Why is plumbing the better bet?

Leo: Because of where the bottleneck actually sits. The training researchers are deep in model math, and that is genuinely hard, specialized work. But right now the thing blocking the team is not another clever architecture idea. It is that the evals do not run reliably, the hosting link from Borg to GCP is broken, and the setup is unrepeatable. Those are infrastructure problems, and they are precisely what Frank is already strong at, cloud, Terraform, and multi-cloud wiring. So he is not competing on the researchers' turf. He is removing the exact blocker that is freezing the researchers. When you make the evals reproducible and the hosting solid, every model change after that gets evaluated faster and more trustworthy. You are not helping one experiment. You are speeding up all of them.

Maya: So the leverage is that fixing the plumbing multiplies everyone else's work.

Leo: That is it exactly. A single reproducible eval pipeline pays off on every future run, for every engineer, for as long as the project lives. That is why Sara framed evals as the fastest on-ramp from being an infrastructure engineer into real model building. The math is not the door. The plumbing is the door, and it happens to be the door Frank already has the key to.

Maya: The thing I will actually hold onto is the orders database example. Grade the world, not the words. Because the model can say cancelled and never cancel anything, and only the state tells you the truth.

Leo: Hold onto that one. It is the whole philosophy in one image. Grade what the model did to a real backend, run it reproducibly so the number means the same thing every time, and make the hosting solid so the eval can even happen. Do those three, and you have turned evaluation from the team's bottleneck into its steadiest tool.

scene.title: Evals as infrastructure: from brittle document to one-shot runs
scene.description: Orientation for how a compliant Gemma model actually gets evaluated: two eval harnesses (Gbench and Ditto), the Borg to GCP hosting crossover that gates the internal suite, two serving runtimes that must line up, and the move from a fifteen page manual document to reproducible one-shot runs owned as infrastructure.

panels.title: The two eval worlds and where they run
panels.description: Gbench and Ditto are two separate eval harnesses with different jobs and different homes, and the hosting crossover between them is the current blocker.

data.label: Gbench
data.value: academic evals plus agentic tasks in a seeded fake world

data.label: agentic grading
data.value: inspect backend state, grade what the model did

data.label: Ditto
data.value: internal-format runner on Borg, pings a model API

data.label: hosting crossover
data.value: Borg job must reach a GCP-hosted endpoint

data.label: serving surfaces
data.value: same weights through vLLM and Hugging Face

panels.title: Anatomy of a one-shot reproducible run
panels.description: The stages Frank turns the brittle document into: provision, pin, execute, persist, tear down, so every run is identical and reviewable.

data.label: Docker pins code
data.value: image plus a specific Gemmaclaw benchmark commit

data.label: Cloud Run executes
data.value: one job against one model build

data.label: durable storage
data.value: results emitted to outlast the job

data.label: tear down
data.value: environment removed, no idle cost or rot

cues.label: Evals are the bottleneck
cues.headline: A compliant model, but nothing to trust
cues.narration: The model is coming together, yet nothing ships until it can be evaluated, and today the eval setup is a brittle fifteen page document.
cues.transform: try to run it through manual steps

cues.label: Gbench and agentic tasks
cues.headline: A harness that stands up a fake world
cues.narration: Gbench runs academic evals and agentic tasks, seeding fake files and tools, triggering the model as an agent, then inspecting the resulting state.
cues.transform: seed a sandbox and let the agent act
cues.pass: a final backend state to grade

cues.label: Grade the world, not the words
cues.headline: The action is the truth
cues.narration: Following the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard and tau-bench, you grade what the model did to a backend, because the sentence can lie and the state cannot.
cues.transform: check the orders database, not the reply

cues.label: Ditto, Borg, and GCP
cues.headline: The crossover that gates everything
cues.narration: Ditto runs internal-format evals on Borg and pings a model hosted on GCP, and that Borg to GCP hosting link is exactly where Sindhu is stuck.
cues.receive: an internal eval request on Borg
cues.pass: a score, only if the link works

cues.label: Two serving runtimes
cues.headline: Same weights, different behavior
cues.narration: The model is exercised through vLLM and the Hugging Face implementation, and the eval must hit the runtime you actually intend to certify.
cues.transform: serve through vLLM or Hugging Face
cues.pass: the runtime the eval must match

cues.label: One-shot reproducible runs
cues.headline: Infrastructure as code replaces the document
cues.narration: Terraform provisions, Docker pins the code and a benchmark commit, Cloud Run executes one job, results land in durable storage, then everything tears down.
cues.transform: provision, pin, execute, persist, tear down

cues.label: The highest-leverage on-ramp
cues.headline: Fix the plumbing, speed up everyone
cues.narration: Making evals reproducible and the hosting solid unblocks every future model change, which is how an infrastructure engineer plugs into model building.
cues.transform: remove the eval and hosting blocker
cues.pass: faster, trustworthy evaluation for the whole team
